issues.description: Module 7 is placed under 'College Board Unit Six, Gene Expression and Regulation.' In the current CED, Unit 6 IS 'Gene Expression and Regulation' and DNA structure/replication does sit within it, so this is acceptable — but the narration should clarify that DNA replication is a foundational topic at the start of Unit 6 rather than implying the whole unit is about replication.

The '~6 feet / 2 meters per cell' figure is the standard textbook value but is a slight oversimplification — that is the total contour length of all 46 chromosomes in a diploid human cell. Fine for AP, but worth noting it is per diploid genome, not per chromosome. Also, '1 error per billion bases' is the post-proofreading + post-mismatch-repair rate. Polymerase proofreading alone gets ~1 in 10^7–10^9; pairing it with the next-section claim is OK but section 13 attributes 1-in-10^9 to proofreading alone, which is on the high end.

Watson/Crick/Franklin attribution is acceptable but understates Franklin: she did not merely 'take' Photo 51 — her quantitative measurements of the helix parameters were used (without her consent) by Watson and Crick. AP graders increasingly expect Franklin credited as a co-discoverer, not as a photographer. Also Maurice Wilkins is missing (he shared the 1962 Nobel; Franklin had died by then).

'Regulatory proteins read the sequence without ever unwinding the helix' is true for many transcription factors but overstated as a universal — some sequence-specific binding requires local distortion. For AP it's acceptable shorthand.

Description of 5' and 3' is correct in spirit but slightly imprecise. The 5' phosphate is attached to the 5' carbon of the sugar; the 3' OH is on the 3' carbon. Saying the phosphate 'hangs off the five-prime carbon, and the next nucleotide attaches at the three-prime carbon' is fine, but students often need the explicit 'phosphodiester bond forms between the 3' OH of the growing strand and the 5' phosphate of the incoming nucleotide.' Worth tightening.

Correct on date (1958) and conclusion. Could note specifically that after ONE round all DNA was intermediate density (ruling out conservative) and after TWO rounds half was light/half intermediate (ruling out dispersive). As stated, the script just says 'the pattern matched only one model,' which is correct but loses the actual logic AP FRQs often ask about.
'About ten nucleotides' for the RNA primer is reasonable (typical range cited is 5–10 in bacteria, ~10 in eukaryotes). Acceptable for AP.

'About a thousand to two thousand bases long in bacteria' — Okazaki fragments in E. coli are typically 1000–2000 nt; in eukaryotes they are 100–200 nt. The narration correctly restricts the figure to bacteria, good.

Attributing the 10^-9 error rate to proofreading alone is slightly too generous. Proofreading by the polymerase brings raw misincorporation (~10^-5) down to ~10^-7; mismatch repair after replication brings it to ~10^-9–10^-10. AP graders generally accept '~1 in 10^9' as the overall replication fidelity, so this won't be marked wrong, but it conflates two mechanisms. The mismatch-repair step (mentioned in the CED) is not named in the script — worth adding one sentence.

'Telomerase carries its own RNA template' is correct (it's a ribonucleoprotein/reverse-transcriptase-like enzyme). Good. Cancer claim ('most cancers reactivate it') is accurate — ~85–90% of cancers reactivate telomerase. Fine.

Clean summary of Pol III / Pol I / ligase. Could note that DNA polymerase I also has 5'→3' exonuclease activity (which is HOW it removes the RNA primer) — the script says it 'chews out' the primer in section 12 but doesn't name the activity. For AP this level of detail is not required.

summary: No factual errors that would cost a student points on the AP exam. Several small refinements would tighten the script: clarify that the 10^-9 fidelity is the combined result of proofreading PLUS mismatch repair, add one sentence on the Meselson-Stahl banding pattern logic, and give Franklin (and Wilkins) fuller credit in the discovery story.
